turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: We'll hear argument next in No. 9 Original United States against the State of Louisiana. Mr. Bruce, you may proceed whenever you're ready.

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: This case concerns the location of Mississippi's coast line and the extent of its seaward boundaries. During the course of my argument, I'll be referring to charts which were included in the amicus brief of the State of Alaska, as well as Figure No. 2 included in the Report of the Special Master. Now, in the previous proceeding before this Court, we were all focusing on the question of whether Mississippi Sound constituted inland waters. We presented our case and this Court agreed that it was inland waters and directed the Master to frame an appropriate decree. In approaching that task, the Master was faced with a problem a-s he approached the western end of Mississippi Sound in the area where Mississippi Sound meets the waters of Chandeleur Sound south. The obvious solution the problem was that a corner had to be drawn somewhere closing both of these The Master however felt constrained not to deal with an issue which appeared to be obvious in light of the Court's previous opinion, that is, necessary and obvious for establishing a coast line. Now, the Solicitor General's argument is confusing as to whether or not he is suggesting remand or that this case is at an end and that no further proceeding should be taken. He says this is a case about Mississippi Sound only, and that it should be reserved for a supplemental decree. What we're asking the Court to do today is to remand this case with instructions to the Master so that he can deal with the coastline question in a logical manner and not feel foreclosed from recommending a proper decree in the case. If that's the Solicitor General's position, too, then we're all agreed on remand and there's no issue for this Court to decide. However, if the Government is arguing that the case is over, we contend that they are wrong. Mississippi Sound and Chandeleur Sound to the south are inland waters or at least this —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Bruce, I take it from the way you're pronouncing the word that Louisianans pronounce it "chandelier" sound, even though the last syllable is spelled "leur"?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Yes, sir. Both of these bodies of water have been characterized by this Court as inland waters. In fact, the last proceeding, waters. The two sounds are at right angles to each other and are separated from one another by Cat Island, West Ship Island and East Ship Island. And I think it's very important to note that Ship Island in the past has been a single island.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The only issue in this case is Mississippi Sound, or not?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: No, Your Honor. Basically that was disposed of at our last proceeding before this Court. The question now is where the coastline should be located, that is, should it take into consideration the inland waters of Chandeleur Sound as well.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, why does Chandeleur Sound come into the case?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Well, because the Master in his report was attempting to delineate the coastline of Mississippi. In order to delineate the coastline of Mississippi, you have to determine the limits of inland waters.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, why didn't he consider it?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: He felt that he was foreclosed by this Court's previous opinion.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You have to address our opinion, then. Before Chandeleur Sound is involved, you have to convince us the Master was wrong in construing our remand.

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Well, we think, Your Honor, —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Isn't that right?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, tell me why the Master was wrong in that regard.

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Well, I think first of all in the opinion, the Court noted that this was a very narrow question involving only Mississippi Sound, or at least that's the way I read the very beginning of this opinion. The parties were much agreed that Chandeleur Sound was inland waters. In fact, the United States has offered a concession to a part of Chandeleur Sound.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Which he rejected, which Mississippi rejected?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, because we felt that the offer was less than what we were entitled to —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It's amazing, isn't it.

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: — as a matter of law. And of course the question as to the status of Chandeleur Sound was raised before the previous Master and was presented to this Court in the claim of a straight base line.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, in this proceeding when did the State first raise the Chandeleur Sound issue?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Your Honor, that would have been back, I believe, in early 1983, prior to the time the Master had submitted his previous recommendation. In fact, the Master notes on page 24 of his opinion in his footnote that Mississippi did raise the question of the status of Chandeleur of this Court which have recognized Chandeleur Sound as inland waters. If you go back to the 1906 decision of Louisiana v. Mississippi, the question there was the boundary between the State of Mississippi and the State of Louisiana. I think in that regard, it's interesting to look at what the parties were claiming and specifically what the Court said about Chandeleur Sound. In that case, of course, the State of Louisiana asked for a decree defining its boundary line as the deep water channel. I think the language of that, which the Court repeated, is important. Louisiana asked that the boundary extend through Mississippi Sound, north of Isle Au Pitre, through Cat Island past southwest of Cat Island, through Chandeleur Sound, northeast of Chandeleur Islands to the Gulf of Mexico. Now, very clearly, the Court was under the impression — or at least Louisiana was under the impression that the Gulf of Mexico began eastward the the Chandeleur Islands.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, let's assume you're right. Everybody agrees that whether the Master was right or wrong in saying that the remand was a narrow one, everybody agrees you're going to have to finish up the case anyway. He's going to have to deal with Chandeleur Sound some time. But what about the accuracy of what he did? serious problems with the accuracy.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, isn't that the issue that we have before us? Was he right to the extent that he set the coastline?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: No, Your Honor, he was not. And we submit that for several reasons. The first reason —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, it's one of the issues isn't it? Was he right in setting the coastline to the extent that he set a coastline?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Well, yes, Your Honor, that would be one of the issues for the Court to consider in deciding whether to affirm the Report of the Special Master.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: And if —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And you object to what he did. You say the Master was wrong?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Yes, we do, Your Honor. First of all —■

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And you're going to argue that, I suppose?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. There were a number of things that we object to about the Special Master's report and of course this statement from Louisiana v. Mississippi is an early legal precedent we believe that shows that he is wrong. The Court in its final decree in that case interestingly enough attached a map and made it a part of the boundary line which the Court delineated runs into Chandeleur Sound. In fact, it runs off the paper, because all of Chandeleur Sound was not shown. In addition, the Court commenting on what Louisiana had argued made it very clear as to where Chandeleur Sound began and ended and where the Gulf of Mexico began and ended. The Court said, to get into the open water of the Gulf of Mexico beyond Chandeleur Islands and around to the western boundary of Louisiana, it is necessary to follow, as Louisiana contends, the deep water channel through Mississippi Sound, and thence by the pass between Cat Island and Isle Au Pitre north of the Chandeleur Islands into the Gulf of Mexico. Now, I think it would be very difficult to read that language —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Between Cat Island and Isle Au Pitre?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Yes, sir. North of the Chandeleur Islands into the Gulf of Mexico. So the Court seems to very clearly expressly have stated its opinion that the Gulf of Mexico began beyond the Chandeleur Islands, the northern part of the Chandeleur Islands.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Was that question squarely involved in that case? You know, a Court will write an opinion and it will be focusing on one point, and it may say casually, to get from here into the Gulf of Mexico without really thinking it's saying other than kind of descriptive language about where the

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: I think that could be an argument that would be made to oppose this. However, the issue presented to the Court was the location of the boundary between Mississippi and Louisiana, and of course the Court fond that the boundary of Louisiana went all the way to the Gulf of Mexico. So in order to resolve that, the Court would have had to have decided where the Gulf of Mexico began. So I think it could be argued that way, but I would think that it was material to the determination of the issue and it was the lateral boundary between the two States. One of the other problems that we find with the Master's report, and that is that the recommendation if accepted would divest Mississippi of approximately 150 square miles of territory which the United States has been recognizing for the most part of this century. And which the United States, at this point, has been willing to offer a partial concession. In 1965 in the California case, California attempted to analogize Santa Barbara Channel to Chandeleur Sound and the United States was very quick to respond, these are really two different cases. Chandeleur Sound, which we recognize as inland waters — and then it went on to contrast the two. The Court picked up on that in a footnote and said something to the effect, Chandeleur Sound, which the United States claims as inland waters. So this Court has been Brief. It seems odd in a way that we should be relying on a map which is found only in the Amicus Brief.

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Especially from Alaska.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But take a look at where it shows Chandeleur Sound, and then on the left all that land, is that mainland Louisiana?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Yes, it is, Your Honor. And that was decided of course in Louisiana v. Mississippi in 19 6 as well and the Court held that was the mainland of Louisiana. Isle Au Pitre is the furthest extension of the Louisiana mainland.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: In addition to those problems, we feel that the Master confused the terms, boundary and coastline. Of course, the two are distinct. The boundary is located three miles seaward of the coastline, the coastline being defined as the limit of inland waters, and the low water mark along that Now, by confusing the terms, the Master not only deprived Mississippi of the claims of Chandeleur Sound inland waters, but at the same time, took away all of Mississippi's territorial sea all along its coastal front. Another problem that has been raised is the treatment of the —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Bruce, could I ask you a question about the map, too. As I understand it, the lines that are marked 3 from Petit Bois to East Ship Island, that's not in dispute, right?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: No, Your Honor, that is not.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And how about the lines marked 7 from West Ship Island down to the Isle Au Pitre. Are they in — that's what the Master proposed, and you dispute that? Is that right?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: That's right, Your Honor. We submit that the proper closing line should have been the line marked 6 from East Ship Island down to the northern tip of —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: All right, and from that point, how do you get from there to Isle Au Pitre. What do you think the Master should have done. I understand that line 6 is the one you contend for but that doesn't close anything if you just go down there. Where would you go to the west from that line in your view?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: From the eastern tip of Ship Island, we

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I understand that. But then what do you do once you get to the eastern tip of Chandeleur Island. You don't claim all of Chandeleur Sound, do you?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Oh, no, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But I'm asking you, where do you go from there under your view of the case?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Mississippi would be entitled only to that area north of its lateral boundary of Louisiana.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But Justice Stevens asked you about a line that comes down. Six goes down from East Ship Island to the northern most of the Chandeleur Islands, and then where does the line go as it goes westward from there?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Oh, I see. Your Honor, the line would continue South around the outer extremity of the Chandeleur Islands continue on South and then close across an area which is not shown on this map. Chandeleur Islands the United States has drawn a closing line for its agreement with Louisiana down to the Mississippi Delta.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And you then contend that all of Chandeleur Sound that's shown on this map is part of Mississippi?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: No, Your Honor. No, Your Honor, just that part of Chandeleur Sound north of the lateral boundaries separating Mississippi and Louisiana.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In other words, north of line number 1? Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And I still question how do you get from the northern tip of Chandeleur Islands to the line number 5. I still don't understand.

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Your Honor, I'm not sure I understand.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I haven't been able to understand. I understand you think the line should begin at Petit Bois Island, go to East Ship Island, go south to the northern tip of Chandeleur Islands.

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And then where does it go?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: The coastline would follow the outer fringe of the Chandeleur Islands.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: All right, get down to the south —

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Down to the Mississippi Delta.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: All right, down to the south tip of Chandeleur Islands.

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Whose coastline? But that's not Mississippi's coastline?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: No, Your Honor. That would not be.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what he's asking is where's Mississippi's coastline. I think what you would do is you would head west from the point where line 6 intersects with the dotted line 5. Isn't that where you think the boundary of

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. Basically we would use the line down to the lateral boundary and follow the lateral boundary —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Down over to 1.

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you would close Chandeleur Sound from the south end of the Chandeleur Islands over to the Delta?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And then Chandeleur Sound is divided between Mississippi and Louisiana?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: The northern part of it would be, yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And what about the southern part of Chandeleur Sound, is that all Mississippi?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: No, Your Honor, that wouldn't be. The southern part of Chandeleur Sound and Breton Sounds which have also been recognized as inland waters would belong to Louisiana, there's no question about that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So what's in dispute then is the area to the west of line 6 and north of line 5 and 1, and south of Cat Island and West Ship Island?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That area's in dispute. Now, do you think the Master has decided what State that water is in? what State it's in. I think he's decided what it's not in.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, do you think he has decided it's not in?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He hasn't just said for the purpose of this case, the border should be you know what he's drawn. You don't think it's open to you to relitigate in a new proceeding what the status of that water is?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: It appears from what the Master said that he was fixing our boundary as that term is used and the term coastline is used, that would limit the State as to how far it could go. And he makes the coastline go between the islands.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I understand.

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Does not even give us a three mile limit, so that leaves a long finger of land in there which we estimate to be somewhere around 150 square miles that would be lost to Mississippi. That does create some problems.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, it's a long finger of water that you contend is land. Isn't that all water?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: I'm sorry, yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: The problems that this would raise also under the decree, the 1981 decree in the Louisiana case the Court awarded to Louisiana all of the land west of the closing line of Chandeleur Sound, subject to the location of the

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Let me ask you another question if I may, because I'm finally getting this. You did argue before the Master, and it's referred to I think in footnote 15, isn't that the one?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That this particular area should have been part of your State, Mississippi.

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The Master rejected that and you did not file an exception to that holding of the Master, and we affirmed, and so isn't the law of the case that you're not entitled to this because it was raised and was rejected before the Master on the prior appeal?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: I suppose the Court could view it that way. We did file an exception that is that Mississippi Sound and Chandeleur Sound were inland waters by virtue of the straight face line. The Court did not address that particular issue, but limited itself to the historic bay claim. It did not address the juridical bay claim which the Master raised. Said it was not necessary and decided the case on historic bay. Along with that of course we felt with the case law of the statements by the United States over a long period of time that there was no dispute to the fact that Chandeleur Sound was inland waters and that the only question we had to decide was the closing line between the two. As I say, in the those enclaves and high seas inside Mississippi Sound. That was the thing that took most of the attention. What we're asking the Court to do in this case is remand to the Master so that he can consider and determine the status of Chandeleur Sound as inland waters and recommend an appropriate decree to the Court. In this respect, we specifically request that he be directed to consider evidence of the use of straight base lines by the United States for delimiting Chandeleur Sound and the applicability of Article 4 of the Geneva Convention on the Territorial Sea.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, if you lose on that, you can still bring another original action, can't you?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Your Honor, we would feel that if we lost after being referred back to the Master that another original action would not be permissible. And furthermore, we think that referral back to the Master at this time would not only be saving in time and this area of course is an area people are very much interested in because of the oil and gas leasing activity and we would certainly —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I can understand your second point, not your first. If the Master chooses not to act on Chandeleur Sound, how have you lost as of this point?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: I'm sorry, if the Master?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I think Justice Blackmun is asking, if you lost on that point here -- not if you lost after it's referred Master, you can bring another original action, can't you?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: I think that that would be questionable as to whether we could or not. Certainly, if we did, I think we'd be faced with the same problem that we have with this Master, that he would be reluctant to entertain something that he feels that the Court has already viewed and passed on. And I think that's the real problem we have with the Master's report. He did not consider the evidence of the straight base line use, the long history of claims by the United States, because he felt that this Court in its last opinion had precluded him from making such findings. He said, in view of the Court's previous opinion, the only way this can be inland waters is by agreement, or if the United States subsequently uses the system of straight base lines. So I think the Master would be reluctant to entertain a further action. He would feel if this Court had the opportunity and did not reverse, that —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, he certainly would do it if we asked him to.

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Your Honor?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He certainly would do it if we asked him to.

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: He certainly would.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: May I ask you another question with the map, because I'm intrigued by this now. down to what was at one time the northern tip of Chandeleur Islands which I guess had actually been moved a little bit to the east to point 4. And that would suggest to me that the boundary of the inland waters are from between — under that view of the case -- from West Ship Island to the northern tip or the former northern tip of Chandeleur Islands. What is the possible justification for claiming the area between points 5 and 6?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Lines.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Or lines 5 and 6?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: You mean, why would Mississippi be entitled to anything greater than what the United States has offered?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, no. That's greater than the inland waters as defined in the Louisiana boundary, is it not? Is it not true that the Louisiana boundary goes line 1, line 5, to the intersection with the line descending from West Ship Island?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: All right, now why would one treat anything east of West Ship Island and the northern point of Chandeleur Islands as inland waters? What is the argument.

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Your Honor, we believe that we have evidence and of course the decision in Louisiana v. Mississippi would tend to support that, that this area has also been in its entirety is inland waters. Now, the question you may be asking, and that is, isn't it unusual to have a dog leg, so to speak, with having the boundary drop back. It's not unheard of. In fact, Congress fully anticipated that when they passed the Submerged Lands Act. Florida has a three league boundary. Mississippi and Alabama have a three mile boundary. So does Louisiana. Texas has a three league boundary. So the coastline, as you're going from Florida to Alabama drops in roughly seven miles. Florida and Texas have seven more miles of territorial sea than do the States inbetween. So this is no unusual and Congress certainly made that a possibility.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Bruce, could I ask you a question concerning Figure 2 in the Alaska brief, not Figure 1.

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Am I correct in understanding that if the United States prevails here, there is that strange indentation along the Louisiana line well toward Cat Island. Is that a correct conclusion?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. In fact, it would go all the way to Cat Island. The line that's shown as the three mile territorial sea the Court would have to reject the Master's recommendation as to the boundary before Mississippi would even get the three miles from its mainland. In other words, if the Master's report were accepted, all of that line between Cat Island and Isle Au Pitre north to the Barrier Islands and the lines connection those would be in question. The question is whether it would be inland waters of the State of Louisiana, whether it would be high seas, whether it would be territorial seas of Mississippi partially or would it be partially territorial seas of Louisiana. It would create a jurisdictional nightmare.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It would certainly on its face a seemingly strange result, isn't it.

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, it does. If there are no further questions, I'll reserve the time I have left.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Bruce. We'll hear now from you, Mr. Minear.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The United States urges that this Court reject Mississippi's exceptions and approve the Special Master's report. I would like to begin by stating the procedural context of this present case. Alabama and Mississippi commenced this proceeding in 1979 to define the States' coastline for purposes of the Submerged Lands Act. Mississippi extending from Petit Bois Island to Cat Island and forming the southern most boundary of the State of Mississippi does in fact constitute the seaward limit of inland waters and is the coastline of Mississippi for purposes of the Submerged Lands Act." This appears at page 3 of the Motion, paragraph 12. That statement defined what this case was all about. And if that wasn't enough, there are a number of other statements that Mississippi has made along the way. In the Memorandum supporting that Motion at page 29 they said, and I quote, "therefore, we submit that the Barrier Islands enclosing the Mississippi Sound represents the outer limit of inland waters for the purposes of the Submerged Lands Act. And Mississippi is entitled to have this Court designate its coastline as extending from the line of ordinary low water mark on the seaward side of such islands a distance of three geographic miles into the Gulf of Mexico."

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And the United States claims Mississippi Sound was not a juridical or an historic bay?

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That was the issue.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And the Master and this Court found for the States?

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: That is exactly right, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And so that Mississippi's southern

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor. And in fact, the Master's 1984 report was quite clear on this. "The sole issue raised by these motions is whether the coastlines of Mississippi and Alabama are the line of ordinary low water mark along the southern mainland and along certain islands adjacent thereto, or whether the waters of Mississippi Sound are inland waters and those coastlines are therefore the line of ordinary low water along the southern shore of those islands together with the line marking the seaward limit of those waters." That appears at page 2 of the Special Master's Report. This Court noticed this same point as well in its opinion at page 470 U.S. 96: "The two States, Alabama and Mississippi, contend that the whole of Mississippi Sound constitutes inland waters. Under this view, the coastline of the States consists of the lines of ordinary low water along the southern coast of the Barrier Islands."

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And then they are entitled to three miles beyond that?

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: That is right, Your Honor, and there's no dispute. The Master's Report seems to have misused the word, boundary, for coastline in its conclusions, but there's no dispute of the right to —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So our remand, our most recent remand was just for the purpose of actually defining that line drawn along where did the line run along those islands. we submit that that is what this case is all about.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, now, what if they are right at bottom that Chandeleur Sound is also inland water? Now, if that position is ultimately correct, what does that do to the line up there around the Mississippi Louisiana line? It gets very peculiar.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Your Honor, no. The United States submits if there was a determination that Chandeleur Island is inland water -- and we further submit there has been no such determination.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You mean Chandeleur Sound.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Sound.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Chandeleur Sound, excuse me, was inland water.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Then what happens.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: And the proper line we would submit would be the line indicated in the Alaska map, line 5.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Which, the first map?

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: This would be the first map.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Figure 1.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Of Alaska. And this line which we have offered as a settlement of this case would in fact ensure the continuous coastline between Mississippi and Louisiana, would simply be an extension of the stipulation that we made with the State of Louisiana in 1974. And I should note that in that that Chandeleur Sound was inland water. And this Court noted that concession.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What did you do? Did you make that part of Chandeleur Sound below the line, did you recognize that as Louisiana waters?

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: What our stipulation said, and this appears in the Special Master's 1970 Report at A-2, is that we would stipulate that Louisiana would be entitled to use Chandeleur or it would be its rights under the Submerged Lands Act would be recognized in Chandeleur Sound without an admission on our part that these are inlands waters for international purposes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Where did that southern line close from the southern end of the Chandeleur Islands over to where?

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: The Louisiana agreements in these cases, it would have been or was in fact an extension of the line that marks the Mississippi Louisiana boundary to line 5 which then extends southerly to the northern tip of Chandeleur Island.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Lines 5 and 6 diverge there. Are there two different little islands there? Is that the problem?

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: No, Your Honor. Actually the difference here is there's been some movement of the entire chain of islands as a result of simply accretions and relictions.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You're saying, Mr. Minear, that the Government has offered to agree that the Mississippi boundary

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Yes. We've agreed to recognize this as the lateral boundary between Mississippi and Louisiana, and we don't believe there's any dispute between Mississippi and Louisiana to the extension of that line. But wherever that line might be extended, we would then say that the line demarcating the rights of Mississippi and Louisiana would be line 5 to West Ship Island. Now, this has already been agreed to on the portion below the lateral boundary.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Oh, I see. I see.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you don't concede anything about the nature of Chandeleur Sound?

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: No, not as inland water.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And as long as Mississippi doesn't accept it, you say that the limit of her authority under the Submerged Land Act is three miles outside of those Barrier Islands.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Exactly the relief that they requested.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And if they want to start another action you say let them start it.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor. That is our position.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Would it be more efficient to go ahead and remand now and let the thing be resolved, and why not.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: No, Your Honor, I do not think so.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why not? original action proceeded at a snail's pace, and it was very important at the outset to define what the purpose of the original action is. Now, if we simply remand here, we might be remanding for another five years of litigation. I think it's important for Mississippi to file a Motion for Leave for a further supplemental decree in this case to specify exactly what their claims are and what their theories are, so this Court can make the appropriate reference. Mississippi's position, as you can tell, has been moving throughout this litigation, and I think it's important for them to specify to this Court exactly what relief they're asking.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, they've shown on the map what they want. Why are we in doubt? They've made a claim. You can see the lines right here.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Your Honor, part of the question here too are the theories that they are raising. Now, they're also claiming that this is all — well, let me step back for just a second and answer your question, Your Honor. The actual exceptions that Mississippi has filed here it's not asked for a remand to determine the status of Chandeleur Sound. They have asked for an Order from this Court declaring that Chandeleur Sound is inland waters and directing the Master to draw the appropriate line. Now, that matter still remains to be contested. afternoon what they asked for as a remand.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, but this is not what they requested when they filed their brief and we filed our brief in response. I think that we are entitled, the United States is entitled to submit a response if they wish further proceedings to make clear exactly what will be litigated here, and on what theories. Now, some of the theories that they have advanced have in fact already been rejected by the Special Master. The question of straight base lines, everything that was to be said about straight base lines was said by Louisiana in the 1974 proceeding. The Special Master rejected all those arguments. And this Court affirmed the Special Master's Report. So I think it's important that in any further litigation in this case that we be clear about exactly what our theories are and where we're going from here.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: — to Louisiana, by the way, on that settlement.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Your Honor, I'm afraid I don't understand your question.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, you haven't conceded that legally Louisiana's entitled to what you've agreed to recognize?

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Yes, that's right, Your Honor. And as far as that concession goes, I'm sure that Louisiana would want to hold us to the practical effect of the stipulation we

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I suppose if there were further litigation and you won on the law that Mississippi wasn't entitled to any part of Chandeleur Sound that then Louisiana would just have a better deal on Chandeleur Sound than Mississippi, that's all.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: That's right, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: May I ask you a couple of questions.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: First of all, if we were to affirm the Master's Report and overrule the exceptions, would not the United States be in a position to argue that the boundary had been fixed in this proceeding and is no longer open to litigate the status of the water area immediately south of the line between Cat Island and West Ship Island?

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, the United States would be in a position to make that argument. The Solicitor General has not made a determination whether he would make the argument.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And is it not true that there has not yet been a judicial determination as to whether Chandeleur Sound is or is not inland waters?

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And so is it not also true that there is a possibility that if we knew all the answers to these questions that a) it is inland waters, and b) if it is, the line really inland waters, Mississippi presumably is entitled to the portion north of the Louisiana border, isn't it?

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Your Honor, if there's a problem in that regard, it stems from Mississippi's original allegation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, maybe that's right. I'm just saying that maybe they goofed up procedurally and they may have to stand by what they allege. You may be dead right and you may win. I'm just asking you is that not a possible consequence.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Yes. Under those conditions, yes, Your Honor, that would be a possibility.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And if I were, as a member of this Court concerned about that and wanted to protect their right to proceed, what is the best way to do it? To make clear to the Master that the line isn't final for that purpose, or to go ahead and remand it?

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Your Honor, I think that what this Court should do is simply accept the Special Master's Report and tell him to go forward and draw the line. Mississippi will then have several options. One, it can ask for relief prior to the formulation of the decree to this Court specifying exactly what asking for an expansion of the reference of the Special Master. If it does not wish to do that and waits until the decree is entered, it can file a motion for entry of an additional supplemental decree.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, it seemed easier to me, frankly,

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: The problem again, Your Honor, with a remand is that we'll be giving the Master will have a true lack of direction in terms of a reference. Normally a case is referred to the Master based on the submission of the party. In this case, it was the motion by Mississippi for the entry of a supplemental decree. They stated what their position was, and they certainly had plenty of time to determine what their position might be. Mississippi became a state in 1817. There's 160 years there in which they could make some conclusions about what their southern boundary is. In addition, they had five years prior to the preparation of the Master's Special Report. If there are difficulties here for Mississippi, they are primarily difficulties of their own making.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: This is sort of like after you've lost a case, you move to amend your complaint.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Yes, except here Mississippi would need to move to amend their complaint after they won the case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: After they won the case. They want to win some more.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Yes. That's exactly the situation we're faced with here.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But it would seem to me that it is not at all impossible that one would conclude that Chandeleur Sound is

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: That is absolutely correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And if that were true, they wouldn't get anything more and Louisiana might end up with at least a doubtful claim as the one you're talking about their boundary.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Yes, that is right, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is it a factor also that if this proceeding is expanded, Louisiana would have a rather large stake in it, I take it, would they? Would they want to get in on the fun?

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: I'm sure that they would be interested in participating at least as amicus in this case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: — of the Sound than just rely on your agreement.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: I'm sure that that is the case as well, Your Honor. So as you can see, as your questions indicate, that if there is a further remand here, we are talking about an extensive further litigation, and without any clear guidance as to what the absolute scope of that litigation might be.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What was it, is it U.S. v. Louisiana?

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Yes. The actual litigation for —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And how long has this Special Master been working on it?

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: I think this Master has been approaching 20 years, Your Honor. with him, his son, maybe.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: It might well be that the Special Master is quite willing to be absolved from his responsibilities in this case. I simply do not know.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Maybe we don't want to bring in a newcomer in this problem, either.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, unless you have some further questions.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How did we get in this mess? ‘CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST: Thank you, Mr. Minear. Mr. Bruce, you have four minutes remaining.

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Your Honor, first of all, I'd like to address Justice O'Connor's question about the two islands on the map at the northern tip of the Chandeleurs. The point at which the United States' line stops is not a point on land, it's a point out in the water. The point shown for line 6 where Mississippi's line ends is the northernmost of the Chandeleur Islands. It is a land point and it was one of the reasons why we thought that was a more appropriate line.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Could I ask you, when did you find out about the agreement the United States had made with Louisiana with respect to Chandeleur Sound?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: When I personally?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You claimed that your line was those Barrier Islands?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Initially the —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Isn't that right?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Despite the fact that you knew the United States had made a deal with Louisiana recognizing Louisiana's right to some portion of Chandeleur Sound?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Well, I think that's probably giving us more credit than we deserve. I don't think anybody recognized or they certainly wouldn't have drafted the motion that way.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I would think that rather impressive

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor. Once that error was discovered, we did —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What error? What error?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: The question there as to where the line it be a closing line across Chandeleur Sound. That issue was presented to the Master.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Just on this latest remand?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: No, Your Honor, this was presented to the Master prior to his original recommendation. This was presented to him in brief form back in 1983.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It wasn't in your complaint?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: No, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: When did your first put it in the case?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: It would have been in the early part of 1983. The Master makes note of the fact that we did raise that issue as to the status of Chandeleur Sound as well in one of his footnotes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In this Report or in the prior Report?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: In this Report, I believe it's on page 24 in his footnote. He indicates that Mississippi did raise that question.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But it hadn't been raised at the time we k last remanded to the Special Master?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: Well, not at the time that you referred the matter to him originally, that's correct. There has been no remand in this case except —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I see. Of the original submission?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor. So far as the lateral boundary with Louisiana, I beyond the point at which this Court's decree ended in 1906, so there is a question as to exactly where that would be located. With respect to how long it would take this case, Mr. Minear notes that he would expect five years. I think that would be a very lengthy period of time in view of the documentation and in view of the case authorities which we have to support a case before the Master. Once the case was presented after some preliminary negotiations, it took approximately ten months to get the case ready for submission to the Master. And I think that on remand that we could probably wrap this case up in a year's time. I do not anticipate that it would take longer than that. The Master is intimately familiar with the geography of the area. He's been involved in these sort of cases for, as Mr. Minear says, 20 years. He has written reports before, and I think that he could very well handle this matter without unnecessary delay. I think if we have a new Master, he would have the problem of having to become familiar with perhaps a whole new area of law which he might not have been familiar with before. And I think that is one of the real advantages of remand.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Bruce, do you understand the Government's settlement offer to be still open?

turns.name: JIM R. BRUCE
turns.text: No, Your Honor, I do not. They are understand it to be still open. There have been negotiations back and forth with the United States, and we have been unable to reach any kind of agreement, although at one point, we thought we had.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Bruce. The case is submitted.
